main_summary.summary: Courtland Cox, interviewed by Joe Mosnier on July 8, 2011, at the Library of Congress Jefferson Building, recounts his trajectory from Harlem birth in 1941 and a 1945-1952 childhood in Trinidad through Catholic school in the Bronx, Howard University, and the founding of the Nonviolent Action Group (NAG) with Ed Brown and Stokely Carmichael. Cox describes NAG's Project Awareness programming at Howard (the 1961 Bayard Rustin-Malcolm X debate moderated by Emmett Dorsey, the Norman Thomas thermonuclear-warfare debate, and the James Baldwin-Ossie Davis-John Oliver Killens symposium with Sterling A. Brown), the group's role as Julius W. Hobson's shock troops in Washington and on Route 40, and Howard NAG's differentiation from Nashville-style nonviolence-as-philosophy. He recounts the 1962 Robert Kennedy office sit-in with Stokely Carmichael and Tom Kahn, the 1963 March on Washington dispute over John Lewis's speech, the 1964 Atlantic City MFDP credentials fight, and SNCC's 1965-1966 Lowndes County organizing for independent black political power with Jack Minnis's research, Mr. Black Man pamphlets, and John Hulett's sheriff candidacy. Cox then traces his 1967 Stockholm participation in the Bertrand Russell war-crimes tribunal alongside Jean-Paul Sartre and Simone de Beauvoir, his role as Secretary-General of the 1974 Sixth Pan-African Congress in Tanzania under Julius Nyerere with C.L.R. James as intellectual godfather, the founding of Drum and Spear Bookstore + Drum and Spear Press with Charlie Cobb, Judy Richardson, and Anne and Marvin Holloway, and his reflections on King's assassination, the 14th Street rebellion under Mayor Walter E. Washington, and the unresolved economic dimension of the freedom struggle.
key_themes: Howard NAG and SNCC student organizing
main_summary.historical_significance:
Cox is one of the few surviving SNCC central-committee figures who connected Howard University student organizing, southern field work in Lowndes County, the Atlantic City MFDP delegation, and the post-1968 turn toward Pan-African political economy. His firsthand account of the John Lewis March on Washington speech negotiation (with Jim Forman and A. Philip Randolph behind the Lincoln Memorial), the Bob Moses-Bob list incident at Atlantic City, and his role as Secretary-General organizing the 1974 Sixth Pan-African Congress in Tanzania places him at the hinge points where SNCC's faith in federal-rules legitimacy collapsed and where the movement reoriented toward Black Power, international alliance, and economic infrastructure.

The interview is also a rare source for the 1961 Cramton Auditorium Rustin-Malcolm X debate (moderated by Howard government professor Emmett Dorsey), the early NAG-Nashville philosophical divide over nonviolence as tactic versus way of life, and the McComb FBI-protect-evidence-not-people exchange that shaped SNCC's view of federal inaction. Cox's testimony on FBI, CIA, and IRS surveillance after his Bertrand Russell tribunal participation, including his 1970 detention at Heathrow, documents the intelligence-state response to internationalist movement leadership.

chapters.title: From Harlem to Trinidad and Back
chapters.summary: Cox describes being born in Harlem in 1941, raised in Trinidad from 1945 until his grandmother died in 1952, and returning to a New York where his mother sent him to Catholic school, the Trinidad family's deep expectation of college setting him apart from a peer group decimated by drugs.

chapters.title: Two Worlds in the Bronx
chapters.summary: He recalls hiding his books coming home to live in both the world of school and the world of the projects, the respect his peers gave his difference, an absent father, and an awareness of segregation and a jazz-scene culture absorbed from older men, though no organized politics yet.

chapters.title: Arriving at Howard
chapters.summary: Following a cousin to Howard, Cox arrived without a clear plan but with a sense of right and wrong sharpened by Emmett Till and Montgomery, into a Washington as segregated as any in housing, the police, and the department stores in a way New York had not been.

chapters.title: Founding the Nonviolent Action Group
chapters.summary: Cox describes forming the Nonviolent Action Group, picketing Glen Echo Park, RFK Stadium, and segregated Route 40 as Julius Hobson's shock troops, and creating Project Awareness, whose first events were the Malcolm X and Bayard Rustin debate, a Norman Thomas debate, and a Baldwin symposium that drew Sidney Poitier.

chapters.title: The Genius of Why Not
chapters.summary: He recalls NAG run by three large egos, himself, Stokely Carmichael, and Ed Brown, the trips to coordinate with SNCC, and what he now sees as the genius of those seventeen-to-twenty-two-year-olds: moving from asking why the situation existed to asking why not change it, without waiting on those who created it.

chapters.title: Malcolm X and Bayard Rustin at Cramton
chapters.summary: Cox recounts the segregation-versus-integration debate in Cramton Auditorium, Professor Dorsey moderating, Malcolm X giving Bayard Rustin's spare minutes back to himself and delivering a message-to-the-grassroots speech with phenomenally disciplined followers, an event that gave NAG a new cachet at Howard.

chapters.title: Nonviolence as a Tactic, Not a Faith
chapters.summary: He lays out the early tension between the national group that held nonviolence as a philosophy and a way of life and the Howard group that held it as a tactic, his own view that you might as well appeal to an oppressor's liver as his heart, since the other side knew exactly what it was doing.

chapters.title: What to Expect From the Federal Government
chapters.summary: Cox recalls expecting little of the federal government beyond individuals like John Doar and Burke Marshall, and the FBI agent in bombed McComb who told them they were there to protect the evidence, not the workers, and admitted his own two guns left him scared to death.

chapters.title: Going Limp in Robert Kennedy's Office
chapters.summary: He recounts the 1962 sit-in with Stokely Carmichael and Tom Kahn in Robert Kennedy's office, Kahn calling the press from the office, and being dragged out limp until Stokely got up mid-drag to retrieve something and returned to his exact spot, deadly serious work he now recalls with a smile.

chapters.title: Changing John Lewis's Speech
chapters.summary: As SNCC's steering-committee representative Cox describes the March on Washington fight when Archbishop O'Boyle threatened to pull out over John Lewis's speech, their first impulse to tell him to go to hell, and how A. Philip Randolph's appeal to hold the coalition led them to soften the speech behind the Lincoln Memorial.

chapters.title: Playing by the Rules at Atlantic City
chapters.summary: Cox recounts going to Atlantic City believing that presenting the facts would matter, Johnson's diversionary press conference over Fannie Lou Hamer's testimony, the suited SNCC workers lobbying the credentials committee, and the congressman who took their list of supporters and handed it to the Johnson forces to pick them off.

chapters.title: When Power Trumps the Rules
chapters.summary: He describes the two-seats compromise and the whole liberal establishment, from the unions to Martin King to Joseph Rauh, urging the delegation to accept it, the refusal, and the lesson that when it comes to power the rules do not obtain, a moment that pushed many to disengage from the electoral process.

chapters.title: From Protest to Power
chapters.summary: Cox calls Lowndes County the work he is proudest of, where an 80 percent Black county had four registered voters, and explains the turn after Atlantic City from asking others to follow the rules to manipulating an Alabama law that let them build their own county party and seize power.

chapters.title: Comic Books and Mr. Black Man
chapters.summary: He describes Jack Minnis's brilliant research into the county offices, the comic books that taught the roles and responsibilities and showed the candidates' faces, and the Mr. Black Man piece he made with Jennifer Lawson around a Mississippi woman's line that Black people had used their mouths to eat and say yes, and it was now time to say no.

chapters.title: Mr. Jackson's Gun and Taking the County
chapters.summary: Cox recounts the landowner Mr. Jackson who gave them his house and stayed up nights with his gun, the argument that the answer to a brutal sheriff is a new sheriff, and convincing John Hulett and others to run until they could take every office, asking what it profits a man to gain the vote and not control it.

chapters.title: Living With Fear in Lowndes
chapters.summary: Coming in after Jonathan Daniels was killed and Ruby Sales nearly so, Cox describes SNCC's trademark of living where they worked in a house with a hole in the roof, a high-speed chase down dirt roads, being the only person of color at the Wilkins trial for Viola Liuzzo's killing, and telling himself not to be paralyzed by fear.

chapters.title: Carmichael, Lewis, and Black Power
chapters.summary: He traces SNCC growing more militant after Atlantic City, Stokely Carmichael replacing John Lewis as reflecting the mood of younger members, and how Black Power, raised on the Meredith march, drew condemning editorials and the false framing of a good early SNCC and a bad later one.

chapters.title: Organize Your Own Community
chapters.summary: Cox explains the much-misunderstood decision at the Peg Leg Bates meeting, framed by Jim Forman as sending white organizers to work in the white community where the problem lay, his own remark that racism was necessary but not sufficient, the hurt it caused white members, and the funding it cost.

chapters.title: The Russell Tribunal in Stockholm
chapters.summary: Cox recounts SNCC's early anti-war stand and the argument that civil rights people had no right to speak on Vietnam, and being sent to the Bertrand Russell War Crimes Tribunal, the London dinner with Sartre, Simone de Beauvoir, and the European intellectual heavyweights deferring to Sartre like a pope.

chapters.title: The War of Genocide, and the Watch Lists
chapters.summary: He describes pressing the Vietnamese Colonel Ha Van Lau until he agreed that a war against guerrillas, who offer no infrastructure to attack but the people themselves, becomes by definition a war of genocide, the cluster bombs, and the FBI and CIA lists that had him searched, detained at Heathrow, and treated as an enemy of the state.

chapters.title: The Sixth Pan-African Congress
chapters.summary: Cox explains how Atlantic City convinced him that economic power was fundamental and that Black communities had no economic infrastructure, leading with C.L.R. James to convene the Sixth Pan-African Congress in Tanzania to link the diaspora's skills with Africa's land and resources and support the liberation movements.

chapters.title: Secretary-General at Thirty-Two
chapters.summary: Named Secretary-General at thirty-two, Cox recounts the tension between state governments and insurgents that kept some Caribbean and Eritrean groups out, persuading heads of state like Sékou Touré, and the cover that C.L.R. James and Julius Nyerere's involvement gave him, though the gathering lacked the infrastructure to sustain its vision.

chapters.title: Drum and Spear
chapters.summary: Settling in Washington, Cox describes founding the Drum and Spear bookstore and press with Charlie Cobb, Judy Richardson, and others to organize information on African and African American history, Palestine, and Vietnam, and the Center for Black Education, a great political venture if never a great business.

chapters.title: The Night King Was Killed
chapters.summary: He recounts hearing of Dr. King's assassination in a struggling Volkswagen, the disbelief, the DC rebellion that was hostile outward yet calm and polite within, Mayor Walter Washington's tanks on 14th Street as another lesson in what power protects, and the further shock of Robert Kennedy's killing.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

chapters.title: A Violent Country
chapters.summary: Cox reflects on King's and Kennedy's deaths revealing a violent country, H. Rap Brown's line that violence is as American as cherry pie, and his own distance, physical and emotional, from the Chicago convention, which he saw as more about Vietnam and a government that would not listen.

chapters.title: What Was Radical Was Who We Included
chapters.summary: Cox closes with the onion he and Stokely discussed, the economic core beneath the political era he dates from 1955 to Obama in 2008, and his argument that nothing they did was radical except who they tried to include, and that the work now is to broaden the economy to all those at the bottom, Black, poor white, and Native American alike.

loc_video.caption: Courtland Cox oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Washington, D.C., 2011 July 08
